case_name: Dana Bowman v. SWBC Real Estate Services, LLC et al.
original_brief_summary: Dana Bowman, a disabled veteran who lost both legs, sued SWBC Real Estate Services and Jordan Foster Construction alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on physical accessibility barriers he encountered while touring their apartment complexes as a prospective tenant. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(1) for lack of standing, finding Bowman failed to plead that he uses a wheelchair or was personally impeded by the barriers, merely reciting statutory accessibility requirements without showing concrete injury to himself.
original_key_holding: The court dismissed Bowman's § 3604(f)(2) claims for lack of standing because he failed to plead that he uses a wheelchair or was impeded by the alleged accessibility barriers during his property tour, instead merely reciting statutory requirements without connecting them to his own injury.
recap_picked_case_name: Bowman v. SWBC Real Estate Services LLC

MEMORANDUM OPINION AND ORDER 
Before the Court is d efendants SWBC Real Estate Services LLC, SWBC 
Rockwall LP, SWBC RW2 LP, Luxia Rockwall Downes LLC (collectively, the “SWBC 
Defendants”), and Jordan Foster Construction LLC’s (“Jordan Foster”) joint motion 
to dismiss.  (Doc. 48).  Defendants move to dismiss plaintiff Dana Bowman’s Second 
Amended Complaint under Rules 12(b)(1) and 12(b)(6)  of the Federal Rules of Civil 
Procedure.  The Court finds that it lacks jurisdiction  and therefore GRANTS the 
Motion and DISMISSES the case without prejudice. 
I. Background 
Plaintiff Dana Bowman is a disabled special forces veteran who lost the lower 
half of both legs during military service and now advocates for disabled veterans.  
Bowman visited the SWBC Defendants’ apartment complexes, the Lexia Rockwall 
Downes Apartments and the Royalton at Discovery1 (the Properties), as a “tester” in

the guise of a prospective tenant.  Bowman toured the Properties and alleges that he 
“observed and encountered ” numerous barriers to access in violation of the Fair 
Housing Act (FHA), 42 U.S.C. § 3604.2  These barriers included curbs without ramps 
on sidewalks between buildings in the complex, toilets and sinks too close to walls, 
narrow doors, and features like thermostats and mailboxes placed too high on walls. 
Bowman says he “was offered a unit for rent,” but his new complaint is still 
silent on whether he  had an interest in renting an apartment or visiting a tenant. 3 
Bowman pleads that he “cannot independently use certain features of the Property,”4  
and claims

MEMORANDUM OPINION AND ORDER 
Plaintiff Dana Bowman is a disabled veteran who visited the defendant 
properties and sued under several Fair Housing Act provisions regarding barriers to 
access for disabled persons in housing.  The defendants moved to dismiss  (Jordan 
Foster Construction LLC at Doc. 29 and the remaining defendants (“SWBC 
Defendants”) at Doc. 25), arguing that Bowman is a mere tester who lacks a concrete 
injury and thus has no standing to bring his claims.   Whether testers have standing 
has been the subject of many court orders as of late.  Those cases that bind this Court 
make a couple of principles clear.  First, t he Supreme Court has found standing on 
receiving bad information when the law forbids the type of unlawful information the 
defendant gave the plaintiff.1  Second, the Fifth Circuit has found no standing when 
a disabled plaintiff sued a hotel regarding barriers in violation of the Americans with 
 
1 Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363, 374 (1982). 
Case 3:23-cv-00970-X   Document 46   Filed 03/14/24    Page 1 of 14   PageID 589

2 
 
Disabilities Act.2  The Fifth Circuit determined that a plaintiff visiting a website to 
check compliance did “not show  enough of a concrete interest in [the hotel’s] 
accommodations to confer standing ” because she only had general plans to visit the 
area.3   
Here, Bowman’s complaint expresses no concrete desire to rent an apartment 
from the defendants or visit a tenant there.  Instead, Bowman alleges he visited the 
property and was offered a unit to rent  but encountered architectural barriers.  He 
claims he was injured by the visit in terms

classification_note: Opinion confirms: Bowman, disabled veteran tester, 3604(f)(2) standing dismissed for failure to plead he uses wheelchair or was personally impeded.
recap_based_brief_summary: Dana Bowman, a disabled special forces veteran (bilateral below-knee amputee), sued SWBC Real Estate Services, related SWBC entities, and Jordan Foster Construction under FHA 3604(f)(1)-(3) based on alleged architectural barriers encountered while touring two apartment complexes as a tester. In its second Memorandum Opinion (Doc. 55, Mar. 11, 2025), the court dismissed the remaining 3604(f)(2) claim without prejudice for lack of Article III standing because the Second Amended Complaint did not plead that Bowman used a wheelchair or that any barrier personally impeded his access.
recap_based_key_holding: Court grants 12(b)(1) motion and dismisses 3604(f)(2) claim without prejudice; an FHA tester must plead personal impediment, not just statutory recitation, to establish concrete Article III injury under TransUnion and Laufer.

case_name: United States v. Noble Homes, Inc.
original_brief_summary: The United States sued Noble Homes, Inc., Guardian Property Management, and Dean Windham alleging that the defendants failed to design and construct 13 four-unit buildings at Windham Bridge and Hampton Court in compliance with the FHA's design and construction requirements under § 3604(f)(3)(C), including inaccessible building entrances, common areas, and units lacking adaptive design features for wheelchair users. The court denied both parties' summary judgment motions because genuine issues of material fact remain as to whether deviations from HUD's accessibility guidelines constituted FHA violations, given competing expert testimony.
original_key_holding: Both parties' motions for summary judgment are denied because genuine issues of material fact exist as to whether deviations from HUD's safe harbor guidelines resulted in violations of the FHA's design and construction requirements; the government's expert established deviations from safe harbors, but defendants' experts provided competing testimony that the units are accessible, creating jury questions.
recap_picked_case_name: Noble v. United States
classification_note: Docket match unreliable (picked "Noble v. United States" 2255 criminal case). No substantive text retrieved.

case_name: Fair Housing Rights Center in Southeastern Pennsylvania v. SJ Lofts, LLC, et al.
original_brief_summary: Fair Housing Rights Center in Southeastern Pennsylvania sued SJ Lofts, Lion Construction LLC, Streamline Entities, and Michael Stillwell alleging that Michener Lofts, a 44-unit apartment building constructed in Philadelphia in 2018, contained design and construction violations that discriminated against people with disabilities. The court denied the motion to dismiss as to the Streamline Entities, finding FHRC alleged sufficient facts to proceed, but dismissed individual defendant Stillwell without prejudice for failing to allege sufficient facts to support alter-ego liability.
original_key_holding: The court denied the motion to dismiss as to the Streamline Entities because FHRC alleged sufficient facts showing their involvement in construction and identified specific design violations, but granted dismissal without prejudice as to Michael Stillwell individually because the complaint contained only conclusory alter-ego allegations lacking sufficient factual detail.
recap_picked_case_name: FAIR HOUSING RIGHTS CENTER IN SOUTHEASTERN PENNSYLVANIA v. SJ LOFTS, LLC
classification_note: Docket name matches but no free documents available on this docket.

case_name: Chelsie Nitschke and Cynthia George v. 326 Welch Partners; Avenue Construction, LLC; Barnett Design Studio, LLC
original_brief_summary: Two disabled Tennessee residents sued the owner, construction company, and architectural firm of a Nashville apartment complex, alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act's design and construction requirements. The defendants admitted most allegations and acknowledged violations of the FHA and Fair Housing Act Accessibility Guidelines, with the court granting judgment on the pleadings and finding defendants liable for failing to design and construct accessible covered multifamily dwellings under § 3604(f)(3)(C).
original_key_holding: The court granted judgment on the pleadings and found defendants liable for violating 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(C) and the ADA by failing to design and construct the apartment complex in compliance with federal accessibility standards.
recap_picked_case_name: Nitschke v. 326 Welch Partners

MEMORANDUM OPINION 
 
I. INTRODUCTION 
This case alleges violations of the Fair Housing Act (“FHA”), 42 U.S.C. § 3601 et seq, at 
an apartment complex in Nashville, Tennessee.  The Defendants 326 Welch Partners and Avenue 
Construction, LLC, have moved for judgment on the pleadings  (Doc. No. 28) , and admit most 
allegations in the Complaint.  Additionally, they admit allegations on liability for these violations.  
Plaintiffs’ motion will be granted and  Defendants are liable for FHA violations.  The Court 
reserves determination of damages and injunctive relief. 
II. STATEMENT 
Cynthia George and Chelsie Nitschke are both residents of Tennessee and have a disability. 
(Doc. No. 1 at 2–3 (¶¶ 8 –9)). They attempted to access the apartment complex but could not due 
to their disability.  
326 Welch Partners participated in the design and construction of the apartment complex, 
hired various contractors to design and build it, and is its owner. (Doc. No. 1 at 3 (¶ 10); Doc. No.

Barnett Design Studio, LLC, (“BDS”) is also named as a defendant and provided “certain 
architectural services with respect to” the apartment complex (Doc. No. 12 at 3 (¶ 12)). BDS denies 
it owns or has ever owned any interest in the apartment complex  and denies that they are its 
apartments. (Id. at 5 (¶ 27)). BDS has not participated in this motion.

classification_note: Opinion confirms: judgment on pleadings, defendants admitted most allegations, liable for FHA 3604(f)(3)(C) and ADA violations.
recap_based_brief_summary: Two disabled Tennessee plaintiffs sued the owner (326 Welch Partners), construction company (Avenue Construction), and architectural firm (Barnett Design Studio) of a Nashville 129-unit apartment complex for FHA design-and-construction violations. In Memorandum Opinion (Doc. 49, Feb. 12, 2026), the court granted plaintiffs judgment on the pleadings as to 326 Welch Partners and Avenue Construction, holding defendants liable for 3604(f)(3)(C) violations at covered multifamily dwellings; Barnett did not join the motion and later separately settled. Damages and remedies were reserved for mediation.
recap_based_key_holding: Where defendants admit covered-multifamily status, admit non-compliance with FHAAG/ADA standards, and concede entitlement to judgment, Rule 12(c) judgment on liability is appropriate under 3604(f)(3)(C) and 24 CFR 100.205.

case_name: David DeBoard v. Ventry Apartments, LLC, Domo Development Company, LLC, Sunstone Construction Company, LLC and The Ventry Apartments, LLC
original_brief_summary: David DeBoard, a professional fair housing tester who uses a wheelchair, sued Ventry Apartments and related entities alleging the recently constructed apartment complex contained design and construction barriers that violated FHA accessibility requirements. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss for lack of Article III standing, holding that under TransUnion, a professional tester who never intended to rent and alleges only a statutory violation as injury lacks the concrete and particularized injury required for standing.
original_key_holding: A professional tester who never intended to rent at the apartments and alleges only a statutory violation as injury lacks Article III standing to sue under TransUnion LLC v. Ramirez, which requires concrete injury-in-fact beyond the mere fact of a statutory violation.
recap_picked_case_name: DeBoard v. Ventry Apartments, LLC

UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT 
NORTHERN DISTRICT OF INDIANA 
FORT WAYNE DIVISION 
 
DAVID DeBOARD,    ) 
      ) 
 Plaintiff,    ) 
      ) 
v.      ) Cause No. 1:22-CV-396-HAB 
      ) 
VENTRY APARTMENTS, LLC, DOMO ) 
DEVELOPMENT COMPANY, LLC,  ) 
SUNSTONE CONSTRUCTION   ) 
COMPANY, LLC AND THE VENTRY  ) 
APARTMENTS, LLC   ) 
      ) 
 Defendants.    ) 
 
OPINION AND ORDER 
 
 In June 2023, this Court dismissed Plaintiff’s case, finding that he lacked Article III 
standing. (ECF No. 42). At Plaintiff’s request, the Court vacated its judgment against Plaintiff and 
stayed all proceedings, hoping that the United States Supreme Court would provide guidance. 
(ECF No. 49). It did not. Now, after reviewing the parties post-SCOTUS briefs (ECF Nos. 56, 57), 
the Court must again decide whether Plaintiff can establish his Constitutionally mandated standing. 
The Court finds that he can’t, so judgment will again be entered in Defendants’ favor. 
I. Factual and Procedural Background 
A. Plaintiff’s Pleadings 
 The Court incorporates by reference the discussion of Plaintiff’s factual allegations from 
its June 2023 Opinion and Order. (ECF No. 42 at 1-2).  
 In the meantime, Plaintiff moved for leave to amend his complaint with a proposed 
amendment. (ECF No. 45 ). The amendment largely hoes the same factual row as the original 
complaint and states the same cause of action. (Compare ECF Nos. 1, 45 -1). But to establish 
USDC IN/ND case 1:22-cv-00396-HAB-SLC     document 58     filed 04/09/24     page 1 of 4

2 
 
Article III standing, Plaintiff has added injuries. He now alleges that Defendants’ conduct caused 
“frustration, physical difficulty, indignation, and emotional distress,” and that he incurred “out-of-
pocket costs” to visit the allegedly offending apartments. 
B. Acheson Hotels, LLC v. Laufer 
 The guidance the Court hoped for was to have come from the Supreme Court’s decision in 
Acheson Hotels, LLC v. Laufer, 601 U.S. 1 (2023). There, the Supreme Court had granted

classification_note: Opinion confirms: professional tester, TransUnion analysis, Seventh Circuit precedent, no concrete injury -> dismissal.
recap_based_brief_summary: David DeBoard, a self-identified professional FHA tester using a wheelchair, sued Ventry Apartments, Domo Development, and Sunstone Construction for design-and-construction barriers. In Opinion and Order (Doc. 58, Apr. 9, 2024), N.D. Ind. dismissed the case a second time for lack of Article III standing even after DeBoard amended to plead frustration, physical difficulty, indignation, emotional distress, and out-of-pocket tour expenses. Seventh Circuit precedent (Wadsworth, Patterson v. Howe) foreclosed emotional injuries; out-of-pocket costs were self-inflicted bootstrap injuries under Clapper and Moore v. Wells Fargo.
recap_based_key_holding: Post-TransUnion, a professional tester cannot establish Article III standing by adding emotional-distress or self-incurred-cost allegations where the plaintiff never intended to rent and the alleged injury is manufactured to find statutory violations.

case_name: Rosanna Barrera v. LDG Riverstone LP, ET AL.
original_brief_summary: Rosanna Barrera, a tenant with mobility impairments, sued LDG Riverstone LP and related entities alleging the apartment complex's design lacked accessible routes from the parking garage to her unit and that defendants denied her request for a reserved accessible parking spot in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The magistrate recommended dismissing her Section 1983 constitutional claims for failing to plead state action, but found she adequately stated claims under the FHA for design and construction violations and failure to accommodate, allowing those FHA claims to proceed past in forma pauperis screening.
original_key_holding: The magistrate recommended dismissing plaintiff's Section 1983 claims for failure to plead state action, but found she stated non-frivolous FHA claims for design and construction violations under § 3604(f)(3)(C) and for failure to accommodate under § 3604(f)(3)(B) by denying her request for an accessible parking spot.
recap_picked_case_name: Barrera v. LDG Riverstone LP

classification_note: Magistrate R&R confirms: FHA claims allowed past 1915(e) screening, Section 1983 claims dismissed for lack of state action.
recap_based_brief_summary: Rosanna Barrera, a pro se mobility-impaired tenant, sued LDG Riverstone LP, LDG Development, Solidago Residential Services, and Xpert Design & Construction alleging (i) FHA design-and-construction violations (no accessible route from the shared parking garage to her unit), (ii) failure to accommodate (denial of reserved parking spot), and (iii) Section 1983 conspiracy claims against defendants and the Texas Workforce Commission Civil Rights Division. Magistrate R&R (Doc. 4, Dec. 9, 2025) recommends dismissing the Section 1983 claims as frivolous (no state action) but allowing FHA 3604(f)(3)(C) and (f)(3)(B) claims to proceed past 1915(e) screening.
recap_based_key_holding: Pro se plaintiff adequately pleads FHA design-and-construction and failure-to-accommodate claims under 3604(f)(3)(C)-(B); Section 1983 claims dismissed for failure to allege specific facts showing agreement between private and state actors.

case_name: Clover Communities Beavercreek, LLC v. Mussachio Architects P.C.
original_brief_summary: Seven fair housing organizations settled claims with property owners regarding alleged FHA design-and-construction violations at senior housing complexes; the property owners then sued the architects for those assigned claims. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that FHA claims are assignable and the single-satisfaction rule does not bar the claims at this stage, while dismissing abandoned state fair housing claims.
original_key_holding: The court held that FHA claims are assignable under federal law and that the single-satisfaction rule does not bar the assigned claims at the motion to dismiss stage, as factual questions remain regarding whether the original plaintiffs were fully compensated; the court dismissed only the state fair housing claims which plaintiffs abandoned.
recap_picked_case_name: Flocast, LLC. v. Movi Family, LLC.

case_name: Lisa Sallaj v. Calvin L. Tate and Schatten Properties
original_brief_summary: Lisa Sallaj sued her former landlord Schatten Properties and its CEO claiming violations of the FHA, alleging she requested a designated disabled parking space as a reasonable accommodation but defendants failed to ensure the space remained available for her use, causing her to park far from her apartment where she tripped and was injured, and that defendants subsequently initiated eviction proceedings after she hired an attorney to address the accommodation denial. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the complaint stated plausible claims for reasonable accommodation denial and FHA interference.
original_key_holding: The court found plaintiff alleged sufficient facts to state plausible FHA reasonable accommodation and interference claims where she requested designated disabled parking, defendants failed to ensure the space was available for her use, and defendants subsequently initiated eviction proceedings after plaintiff hired an attorney.
recap_picked_case_name: Sallaj v. The Summit Apartments

JUDGE CAMPBELL 
 
   
MEMORANDUM AND ORDER 
 
Plaintiff Lisa Sallaj brings an Amended Complaint against Defendants Calvin L. Tate and 
Schatten Properties under the Americans with Disabilities Act (“ADA”) and the Fair Housing Act 
(“FHA”).1 (Doc. No. 5.) The Court granted Plaintiff pauper status, and the Amended Complaint is 
now before the Court for initial review. The Court must review and dismiss any in forma pauperis 
complaint if it is frivolous, mali cious, fails to state a claim u pon which relief may be granted, or 
seeks monetary relief against a defendant who is immune from such relief. 28 U.S.C. § 
1915(e)(2)(B); see also Ongori v. Hawkins, No. 16-2781, 2017 WL 6759020, at *1 (6th Cir. Nov. 
15, 2017) (“[N]on-prisoners procee ding in forma pauperis are st ill subject to the screening 
requirements of § 1915(e).”).

Hill v. Lappin, 630 F.3d 468, 470-71 (6th Cir. 2010), by viewing the Amended Complaint in the

classification_note: Opinion CONFIRMS core FHA holding (plausible RA + interference claims). BUT: procedural_posture misclassified as MOTION_TO_DISMISS; actual posture is § 1915(e) initial IFP screening. Also: ADA claims were DISMISSED (not mentioned in database). Outcome = initial screening survival, not merits win.
recap_based_brief_summary: Lisa Sallaj, a pro se IFP plaintiff with permanent disability, sued former landlord Schatten Properties and CEO Tate for (1) failing to ensure her designated disabled parking space remained available, causing her to park farther away where she tripped and sustained permanent knee damage, and (2) initiating eviction proceedings after she hired counsel over the accommodation denial. In Memorandum and Order (Doc. 11, Dec. 8, 2022), conducting 1915(e) initial screening, Judge Campbell found Sallaj stated plausible FHA reasonable-accommodation and 3617 interference claims but DISMISSED her ADA claims because defendants are not a covered entity/employer under Title I and apartment common areas are not places of public accommodation under Title III (citing Collins v. PRG).
recap_based_key_holding: Pro se complaint states colorable FHA 3604(f)(3)(B) accommodation and 3617 interference claims where plaintiff requested designated accessible parking, landlord failed to reserve it, she was injured, and was then subject to eviction threats after retaining counsel; ADA Title I/III claims dismissed for absence of covered-entity status and non-applicability to residential apartment common areas.
discrepancy_nature: Database procedural_posture is MOTION_TO_DISMISS; actual posture is Section 1915(e) IFP initial review (no motion to dismiss had yet been filed). Database outcome PLAINTIFF_WIN overstates what happened; this was mere screening-stage survival, not a merits ruling. Database brief_summary and key_holding omit that the ADA claims were dismissed entirely.
recommended_reclassification: Change procedural_posture to IFP_SCREENING (or add this enum value); change outcome from PLAINTIFF_WIN to MIXED (FHA survives, ADA dismissed) or SCREENING_SURVIVAL; revise brief_summary to note ADA dismissal.

case_name: Sarah Brockway v. Arlene L. Petschke Trust No. 102
original_brief_summary: Sarah Brockway sued her former landlord (Arlene L. Petschke Trust No. 102) alleging the landlord violated the Fair Housing Act by failing to accommodate her Ehlers-Danlos syndrome mobility disability. The court denied Defendants' motion for summary judgment, holding that a reasonable jury could find the October 12 Letter provided adequate notice of her disability and accommodation needs for handicap parking, handrails, and ramps, distinguishing Watters because Plaintiff requested disability-specific modifications that a non-disabled tenant would not typically request and Defendants completely ignored the request.
original_key_holding: A reasonable jury could find that the October 12 Letter gave Defendants sufficient notice that Plaintiff had a disability and sought accommodations for it (handicap parking, handrails, ramps), distinguishing this case from Watters where the requested accommodation (privacy fence) could be desired by non-disabled persons and the defendant HOA actually responded to the request.
classification_note: No RECAP docket found. Case is 2025 filing; may not yet be in RECAP.

case_name: Christopher Whiteaker v. City of Southgate
original_brief_summary: Whiteaker sued the City of Southgate, Michigan under the FHA and PDCRA, alleging the City denied his requests for a waiver of Ordinance 610.13 (prohibiting chickens) to keep six emotional support animals for his depression and anxiety. The court denied the City's motion for summary judgment, finding triable issues of fact on both the reasonableness and necessity elements of the reasonable accommodation claim.
original_key_holding: Genuine issues of material fact remain as to whether Whiteaker's requested accommodation of keeping six ESA chickens is both reasonable (balancing City burden against Whiteaker's therapeutic benefit) and necessary to afford him equal opportunity in housing use and enjoyment.
recap_picked_case_name: Whiteaker v. Kuspa

      United States District Court Judge 
 Defendants. 
____________________________/ 
 
OPINION & ORDER 
DENYING DEFENDANT’S SUMMARY JUDGMENT MOTION 
   
  
Plaintiff, Christopher Whiteaker (“Whiteaker”) filed this action against Defendant, the City 
of Southgate, Michigan (“the City”) for viol ations of the Fair Housing Act (“FHA”) and 
Michigan’s Persons with Disabilities Civil Ri ghts Act (“PDCRA”). (E CF No. 15). The matter 
currently before the Court is the City’s Motion for Summary Judgment, brought pursuant to F
ED. 
R. CIV. P. 56. (ECF No. 25). The parties have briefe d the issues and the Court held a hearing on 
December 15, 2022. For the reasons set forth below the Court DENIES the City’s Motion for 
Summary Judgment because Defendant has failed to carry its burden of proving no genuine issue 
of material fact.   
BACKGROUND 
On January 4, 2022, Whiteaker co mmenced this action in th is court. (ECF No. 1). 
Whiteaker filed his Amended Complaint on April 21, 2022. (ECF No. 15). As such, that pleading 
superseded and replaced the original complaint. The City filed its Motion for Summary Judgment 
on August 26, 2022. (ECF No.25). Whiteaker filed his response to the Motion for Summary 
Judgment on September 15, 2022 (ECF No.26), and the City filed its reply on September 22, 2022. 
Case 2:22-cv-10011-SFC-DRG   ECF No. 33, PageID.709   Filed 01/19/23   Page 1 of 11

2 
 
(ECF No. 29). In the present Motion for Summary Judgment, the parties still disagree as to several 
underlying facts. (ECF No. 25 and 26). 
The Amended Complaint alleged that the City violated the FHA (Count I) and the 
PWDCRA (Count II) by denying Wh iteaker’s request for an ex emption from City Ordinance 
610.13, which prohibits City residents from maintaining chickens on their property. (ECF No. 15).  
Fro

classification_note: Opinion confirms: ESA chickens, Ordinance 610.13, SJ denied on reasonableness and necessity elements.
recap_based_brief_summary: Christopher Whiteaker sued the City of Southgate, MI under the FHA and Michigan PDCRA after the City denied his successive requests for a waiver of municipal Ordinance 610.13 (prohibiting fowl) to keep six chickens as emotional-support animals for his Major Depressive Disorder and Generalized Anxiety Disorder. In Opinion & Order (Doc. 33, Jan. 19, 2023), the court denied the City's summary-judgment motion, finding triable fact issues on both (a) whether a waiver is reasonable given the balance between municipal burden (public-health/nuisance concerns) and the therapeutic benefit to Whiteaker and (b) whether the accommodation is necessary to afford equal opportunity in the use and enjoyment of his home.
recap_based_key_holding: Genuine issues of material fact preclude summary judgment on both reasonableness and necessity prongs of an FHA/PDCRA reasonable-accommodation claim where plaintiff seeks a zoning-ordinance waiver to keep six ESA chickens and defendants failed to carry their burden under Anderson v. City of Blue Ash.

case_name: Group Home on Gibson Island, LLC v. Gibson Island Corporation
original_brief_summary: Plaintiffs Group Home on Gibson Island and Assisted Living Well Compassionate Care sued Gibson Island Corporation seeking a reasonable accommodation to waive restrictive covenants prohibiting business use, so they could operate an assisted living group home for disabled seniors on the private island community. The court granted GIC's motion for summary judgment, finding plaintiffs failed to demonstrate that removing four disputed conditions from the proposed accommodation agreement was 'necessary' to provide disabled residents equal housing opportunity under the Fair Housing Act.
original_key_holding: Plaintiffs failed to establish that removing the four disputed conditions in the accommodation agreement (Guarantor Provision, Dispute Resolution Provision, Septic Provision, and Arbitration Escrow Provision) was 'necessary' to provide disabled residents an equal opportunity to use and enjoy the Banbury Property under the reasonable accommodation analysis.
recap_picked_case_name: Selective Insurance Company of South Carolina v. Assisted Living Well Compassionate Care LLC
classification_note: Picked match was unrelated Selective Insurance case. No substantive text retrieved for actual case.

case_name: Women's Elevated Sober Living L.L.C. v. City of Plano
original_brief_summary: Women's Elevated Sober Living LLC and its owner sued the City of Plano challenging the denial of a zoning accommodation request to allow 15-20 residents in their sober living home beyond the eight-person limit in the SF-7 zoning ordinance. The Fifth Circuit vacated the district court's finding of an FHA violation, holding that the district court improperly applied a standard accepting that therapeutically beneficial treatment constitutes necessity under the FHA, when plaintiffs must instead prove the accommodation is indispensable to recovery.
original_key_holding: The Fifth Circuit held that the district court applied the wrong legal standard by accepting that proof of greater therapeutic benefit from larger occupancy was sufficient to establish necessity under § 3604(f)(3)(B); rather, plaintiffs must prove the requested accommodation is indispensable or essential—not merely preferable—to ameliorate their disabilities.
recap_picked_case_name: Women's Elevated v. City of Plano
classification_note: RECAP docket exists and matches case name but no PDF text extracted (5th Cir. appeal — underlying opinion likely published in Federal Reporter).

case_name: Brandon C. Jones v. Ms. Jisin H. Thomas; Mr. Jonathan Tauarez; Volunteers of America Corporation; Volunteers of America, Swartz Shelter; Volunteers of America 135 W. 50th Street, NYC, NY 10024
original_brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Brandon Jones sued Volunteers of America and its employees alleging they violated the ADA, Fair Housing Act, and his constitutional rights by failing to provide reasonable accommodation and retaliating against him at a homeless shelter. The court dismissed the § 1983 and Title VII claims, and dismissed the FHA claim with leave to amend because plaintiff failed to plead facts establishing he has a disability or identifying the specific accommodation defendants denied.
original_key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation claim because he failed to allege any facts suggesting he has a disability within the meaning of the FHA and failed to allege facts regarding the nature of any specific reasonable accommodation that defendants denied.
recap_picked_case_name: Jones v. Thomas
classification_note: RECAP docket matches (Jones v. Thomas, S.D.N.Y.) but only consent order/voluntary dismissal documents; substantive ruling text unavailable.

case_name: Isis Rudolph v. Harrison Metropolitan Housing Authority
original_brief_summary: Isis Rudolph, a pro se plaintiff with PTSD, ADHD, and traumatic brain injury, sued Harrison Metropolitan Housing Authority and Cedar One Property Management under the FHA, ADA, Rehabilitation Act, 42 U.S.C. § 1983, § 1985(3), and state law, alleging denial of reasonable accommodations in her Section 8 voucher program, retaliatory non-renewal of her lease shortly after she requested disability accommodations, and repeated unauthorized entries into her unit. On mandatory § 1915(e)(2) screening of the amended complaint, the magistrate judge permitted the core FHA, Rehabilitation Act, due process, and state-law claims to proceed but recommended dismissal of the ADA claim against Cedar One (private landlord not subject to ADA), the FHA renter's insurance discrimination count (naked assertion of hardship without factual enhancement), and the § 1985(3) conspiracy count (no plausible allegation of coordination between defendants). The court also denied plaintiff's motion to stay eviction proceedings without prejudice and denied her miscellaneous PACER fee-exemption motion as moot.
original_key_holding: On initial § 1915(e)(2) screening, the court allows plaintiff to proceed on her FHA reasonable accommodation, retaliation, Rehabilitation Act, ADA (against HMHA only), due process, and state-law claims, but recommends dismissal of her ADA claim against Cedar One (private landlord is not a public entity), her discriminatory renter's insurance enforcement claim (insufficient factual pleading), and her § 1985(3) civil rights conspiracy claim (vague and conclusory allegations of coordination).
recap_picked_case_name: Rudolph v. Harrison Metropolitan Housing Authority
classification_note: RECAP docket matches (Rudolph v. Harrison Metro HA) but only answers/orders on minor motions; substantive ruling text unavailable.

case_name: Antionette Slaughter v. Valley View I LLP
original_brief_summary: Antionette Slaughter, proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, sued Valley View I LLP (her apartment management company), King County Housing Authority, and other defendants alleging retaliation and discrimination under the Fair Housing Act for filing HUD complaints and requesting reasonable accommodations. The court dismissed her complaint without prejudice for failure to state a claim, finding she failed to plead sufficient factual detail to support her retaliation and discrimination claims.
original_key_holding: The court dismissed Ms. Slaughter's FHA retaliation claim because she failed to provide specific factual allegations regarding when she filed her HUD complaints, the subject matter of those complaints, and a causal link between her protected activity and any adverse action by Valley View.
recap_picked_case_name: Slaughter v. Valley View I LP
classification_note: RECAP docket matches (Slaughter v. Valley View I LP) but no free substantive text available.

case_name: Toni Wiggins v. Omaha Housing Authority Foundation Inc.
original_brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Toni Wiggins sued the Omaha Housing Authority after its CEO called police to have Wiggins and her husband arrested and removed from a property where they were serving as power of attorney and caregivers for a disabled OHA tenant (Henry Lee), and thereafter filed a false eviction action against them. Wiggins alleged violations of the FHA (retaliation under § 3617, disparate treatment/harassment under § 3604), 42 U.S.C. § 1983, 18 U.S.C. § 1001, and various Nebraska statutes. On initial review under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e), the court determined the complaint failed to state any plausible federal claim—finding no factual allegations tying the OHA's conduct to a protected characteristic, no adequate causal link between any protected activity and the adverse actions, and no private right of action under 18 U.S.C. § 1001—and granted Wiggins 30 days to file an amended complaint.
original_key_holding: The court finds that Wiggins' pro se complaint fails to state a plausible claim under the FHA or 42 U.S.C. § 1983 because she does not allege sufficient facts establishing a protected activity, a nexus to a protected characteristic, or a causal link between any protected activity and the OHA's adverse actions, and grants her 30 days to file an amended complaint.
classification_note: No RECAP docket found (2025 case).

case_name: Elewood Torres, on behalf of himself and all others similarly situated v. MMS Group LLC. d/b/a MMS Group, New York City Housing Preservation and Development, New York State Division of Housing and Community Renewal
original_brief_summary: Plaintiff Elewood Torres filed FHA and state law claims on behalf of himself and similarly situated individuals, alleging that Defendants—including property owners, managers, and related entities—failed to maintain two residential buildings to be independently usable by deaf and hearing-impaired persons and failed to provide access to housing for individuals with disabilities. Magistrate Judge Figueredo issued a Report and Recommendation recommending class certification as to most counts with modifications to the proposed class period and class definition. The district court reviewed Defendants' and Plaintiff's objections, applying de novo review to newly raised arguments and clear error review to rehashed arguments, and adopted the R&R in full, certifying the class with the narrowed time period and also granting Defendants' motion to seal sensitive medical information.
original_key_holding: The district court adopted the Magistrate Judge's Report and Recommendation in full, granting class certification under Rule 23 for a class of deaf and hearing-impaired residents and visitors as to Counts Three, Four, Five, Six, Seven, Eight, Ten, and Eleven with modifications narrowing the class period to on or after July 19, 2019 (or 2020 for Count Ten), while denying certification as to Counts One and Two.
recap_picked_case_name: Dawson, on behalf of himself and all others similarly situated v. 1stopbedrooms Inc.
classification_note: No RECAP docket found for Torres v. MMS Group. Retry returned unrelated Dawson class action.

case_name: Pharilyn Chhang v. West Coast USA Properties LLC, et al.
original_brief_summary: Pharilyn Chhang, a Section 8 voucher holder, sued the Housing Authority of the City of Madera (HACM) along with private landlords for denying her request to keep an emotional support dog as a reasonable accommodation for her mental health disability. The Court granted HACM's motion to dismiss, finding that HACM could not be held directly liable for the private landlord's alleged discriminatory conduct because HACM neither owned nor managed the property, lacked substantial control over the private landlord, and did not engage in discriminatory conduct itself.
original_key_holding: The Housing Authority of the City of Madera cannot be held directly liable for the private landlord's refusal to accommodate plaintiff's ESA request because HACM did not own, manage, or substantially control the property, and the form HAP contract did not create the substantial control over the private landlord necessary for liability.
recap_picked_case_name: Chhang v. West Coast USA Properties LLC

case_name: United States of America ex rel. James Dieter and Karen Schwenke v. City of Milwaukee, City of Milwaukee Community Development Grants Administration, Housing Authority of the City of Milwaukee, and Milwaukee County
original_brief_summary: Relators James Dieter and Karen Schwenke sued the City of Milwaukee, Housing Authority of the City of Milwaukee, and Milwaukee County under the False Claims Act, alleging defendants falsely certified compliance with the Affirmatively Furthering Fair Housing mandate and anti-discrimination laws to obtain federal HUD funding. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss with prejudice, finding relators failed to plead falsity with the required particularity under Rule 9(b) and failed to adequately allege a disparate impact theory showing how specific certifications were misleading.
original_key_holding: The court dismissed the False Claims Act complaint with prejudice because relators failed to adequately plead the 'what' and 'how' of falsity under Rule 9(b), failing to tie specific misconduct to specific statutory standards or show how certifications were misleading; relators' focus on only one neighborhood (Near West Side) was insufficient for a disparate impact theory, and their AFFH mandate allegations merely parroted regulatory text without explaining why specific certifications were false.
recap_picked_case_name: United States of America v. City of Milwaukee
classification_note: Picked match was related "U.S. v. City of Milwaukee" case, not the specific qui tam relator action. Cannot confirm identity.

case_name: Yusef Abdullah Bilal Ali v. Louisville Metro Housing Authority
original_brief_summary: Plaintiff Yusef Abdullah Bilal Ali, a public housing tenant with mobility impairments requiring knee surgery, sued Louisville Metro Housing Authority alleging failure to accommodate his requests to keep his emotional support animal off leash and to transfer to a two-bedroom single-family unit. The court denied Plaintiff's Emergency Motion for Injunction Pending Appeal, finding he could not demonstrate likelihood of success on the merits because the off-leash request posed unacceptable safety risks given the dog's documented aggressive history, and LMHA properly denied the transfer request after Plaintiff failed to provide required medical documentation.
original_key_holding: The court denied Plaintiff's Emergency Motion for Injunction Pending Appeal, finding Plaintiff failed to demonstrate likelihood of success on the merits because his requested off-leash accommodation was unreasonable given the dog's documented aggressive behavior, and his transfer request was properly denied because he failed to provide required medical documentation.
recap_picked_case_name: Ali v. Louisville Metro Housing Authority
classification_note: RECAP docket possibly matches (Ali v. Louisville Metro HA) but no substantive documents available.

case_name: Gary Hatter v. Gloria Williams, et al.
original_brief_summary: Gary Hatter, a disabled Section 8 voucher holder, sued the Housing Authority of Champaign County and its employees alleging disability discrimination, racial discrimination, and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act after the agency terminated his rental assistance based on his failure to disclose ownership interest in real estate. The Seventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment for defendants, finding that Hatter's reasonable accommodation request was actually granted, and the subsequent voucher revocation was supported by evidence of program-rule violations unrelated to his disability.
original_key_holding: The Seventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment because Hatter received the accommodation he requested (approval to remain in the two-bedroom unit), so he could not show a denial of reasonable accommodation; his subsequent removal from the voucher program was based on unreported property ownership, not disability discrimination or retaliation.
classification_note: No RECAP docket found for this 7th Circuit appeal.

case_name: Carl William Bass v. John Brannen and Network Property Management
original_brief_summary: Carl Bass, a tenant with chronic fatigue syndrome, sued his landlord John Brannen and property management company alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act, including failure to accommodate his disability-related noise sensitivity and discriminatory lease non-renewal. The court dismissed Bass's failure-to-accommodate claim because the complained-of noises (violin, TV at high volume, barking dog) would affect any tenant regardless of disability, but allowed his disparate treatment claim to proceed based on evidence that Brannen refused to renew his lease after making comments referencing his disability and treated him differently than a similarly-situated tenant who declined the same apartment offer.
original_key_holding: The court dismissed Bass's failure-to-accommodate claim because he failed to show his CFS was the but-for cause of his inability to enjoy his apartment, as the loud noises (screeching violin, TV at volume 70, barking dog) would be intolerable to any tenant regardless of disability; however, his disparate treatment claim survived because Brannen's statement that his building was not a 'convalescent home' and disparate treatment compared to similarly-situated tenant Berger suggested discriminatory intent.
recap_picked_case_name: Bass v. Brannen

case_name: Barbara A. Perricone-Bernovich, et al. v. Community Development Corporation of Long Island, Inc., et al.
original_brief_summary: Plaintiffs Perricone-Bernovich, Bernovich, and John Perricone (who has schizophrenia) sued the Community Development Corporation of Long Island, which administers Perricone's Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher, alleging denial of reasonable accommodation to use the voucher at Perricone-Bernovich's home. The court declined to reach the merits of the FHA claim, instead finding procedural deficiencies: Perricone-Bernovich cannot proceed pro se on her brother's behalf without being an attorney, and neither Perricone-Bernovich nor Bernovich have established Article III standing to assert claims on their own behalf, giving plaintiffs 30 days to amend.
original_key_holding: The court did not rule on the merits of the reasonable accommodation claim; instead, it identified threshold deficiencies requiring plaintiffs to establish capacity (Perricone-Bernovich cannot represent her brother without being an attorney or obtaining a court-appointed guardian) and standing (Perricone-Bernovich and Bernovich have not established their own injury-in-fact sufficient to confer Article III standing).
recap_picked_case_name: Perricone-Bernovich v. Community Development Corporation of Long Island, Inc.
classification_note: RECAP docket name matches but no free documents available.

case_name: Antonelli v. Gloucester County Housing Authority
original_brief_summary: Plaintiff Danielle Lyn Antonelli sued the Gloucester County Housing Authority and its employees alleging violations of the FHA, Rehabilitation Act, ADA, and Title VI based on the downgrading and termination of her Section 8 voucher and the agency's failure to relocate her after domestic violence. The Court dismissed her disability discrimination and reasonable accommodation claims under the FHA, Rehabilitation Act, and ADA for failure to allege a nexus between her disability and the requested accommodations, but declined to dismiss her sex discrimination claim under the FHA based on VAWA violations.
original_key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation claim because her requests for a three-bedroom voucher and emergency transfer were motivated by her son's needs and domestic violence, respectively, not her own disability, thus failing to establish the required nexus between the accommodation and disability; however, the court declined to dismiss plaintiff's sex discrimination claim based on VAWA violations.
recap_picked_case_name: ANTONELLI v. GLOUCESTER COUNTY HOUSING AUTHORITY
classification_note: RECAP docket name matches (Antonelli v. Gloucester County HA) but no free documents available.

case_name: Donavette Ely v. Mobile Housing Board
original_brief_summary: Donavette Ely, a single mother, sued the Mobile Housing Board alleging the Board violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to extend her Section 8 housing voucher as a reasonable accommodation for her son's asthma. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the Board, holding that Ely failed to establish a reasonable accommodation claim because she never informed the Board that she needed a voucher extension to accommodate her son's disability, and the Board therefore could not be liable for refusing to grant an accommodation it did not know was necessary.
original_key_holding: The court affirmed summary judgment for the Board because Ely never explained to the Board that she needed a voucher extension as a reasonable accommodation for her son's disability, and mere knowledge of the son's asthma from a prior bedroom-size request did not obligate the Board to treat her extension request as an accommodation request.
recap_picked_case_name: Donavette Ely v. Mobile Housing Board

recap_based_key_holding: Docket identity confirmed; substantive holding (affirming SJ on ground that Ely never communicated to the Housing Board that her voucher-extension request was a reasonable accommodation for her son's disability) cannot be directly verified from free text but is consistent with the published 11th Cir. disposition.
